1:
"And when the sabbath was past, Mary Magdalene, and Mary the mother of James, and Salome, had bought sweet spices, that they might come and anoint him." The witnesses note the women's devotion and the proof their errand gives of the resurrection. Robertson: "The sabbath having come in between, and now over... It was therefore after sunset... a reverential anointing of the body of Jesus with spices." Barnes draws the evidential point: "This is proof that they did not suppose he would rise again. And the fact that they did not expect he would rise gives more strength to the evidence for his resurrection." Clarke agrees: "this is a proof that they had not properly understood what Christ had so frequently spoken... And this unbelief of theirs is a proof of the truth of the resurrection."

The Latin and Antiochene fathers read the dawn-visit richly. Pseudo-Jerome: "After the sadness of the sabbath, a happy day dawns... in it the chief light shines forth, and the Lord rises in triumph." Theophylact explains the spices' purpose — "that it might remain sweet-smelling... for spices have the property of drying up." Gregory draws the moral: "if we believe on Him who is dead, and are filled with the sweet smell of virtue, and seek the Lord with the fame of good works, we come to His sepulchre with spices."

The puritan Henry: "Never was there such a sabbath... during all this sabbath our Lord Jesus lay in the grave." The women's visit was "not a superstitious one, but a pious one." He notes "the respect which others have showed to Christ's name [Nicodemus's spices], should not hinder us from showing our respect to it." The Reformed Calvin notes the custom of anointing was "applied to a lawful use by the Jews alone... to confirm them in the faith of the resurrection," God representing "the image of life in death."

The witnesses agree: the women, with pious love, bought spices to anoint the body of Jesus — an errand that, while it shows their failure to expect his rising, thereby strengthens the evidence for the resurrection, since they came not as believers in a living Lord but to embalm a dead one. The first day of the week, Henry notes, "is the first day of a new world." The devotion of the women, who came early while the disciples hid, prepared them to be the first witnesses of the risen Christ.
2:
"And very early in the morning the first day of the week, they came unto the sepulchre at the rising of the sun." The witnesses harmonize the apparent discrepancy in the time and note the dawn of the resurrection day. Augustine reconciles the Gospels: "What Luke expresses by very early in the morning, and John by early when it was yet dark, Mark must be understood to mean, when he says, very early in the morning, at the rising of the sun, that is, when the sky was growing bright in the east... when his rising begins to light up the sky." Ellicott: "A morning haze, however, or the partial continuance of the gloom... may well be thought of as harmonizing the two accounts."

Lightfoot, with rabbinic learning, distinguishes the four stages of twilight — "the hind of the morning" (first light), the time when one can "distinguish between purple colour and white," when "the east begins to lighten," and "sunrise" — and matches the four Evangelists' phrases to them. Robertson resolves it simply: "Probably they started while it was still dark and the sun was coming up when they arrived at the tomb. Mark himself gives both notes of time, very early, when the sun was risen." Wesley: "They set out while it was yet dark, and came within sight of the sepulchre... just as it grew light enough to discern that the stone was rolled away... but by the time Mary had called Peter and John... the sun was rising."

Robertson also addresses the timing of the resurrection itself: "Some people are greatly disturbed over the fact that Jesus did not remain in the grave full seventy-two hours. But he repeatedly said that he would rise on the third day and that is precisely what happened. He was buried on Friday afternoon. He was risen on Sunday morning... We can readily understand after three days in the sense of on the third day."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the women came to the tomb at the dawning of the first day, the apparent discrepancy between "while it was yet dark" (John) and "at the rising of the sun" (Mark) harmonized by the gradual lightening of dawn (Augustine) — they set out in darkness and arrived as the sun rose. The first day of the week, the day of resurrection, became the Lord's Day; and the timing fulfilled Christ's word that he would rise "on the third day." The verse marks the moment of discovery, as the women, arriving at first light, found the stone already rolled away (v.4). The dawn of the resurrection day, the fathers note, is the dawn of salvation's new light upon the world. (The Cyril fragment here, on the Church built of the Gentiles, touches the new age the resurrection opens.)
3:
"And they said among themselves, Who shall roll us away the stone from the door of the sepulchre?" The brief verse — peculiar to Mark — records the women's anxious question on the way. Robertson: "Alone in Mark... Clearly the women have no hope of the resurrection of Jesus for they were raising the problem as they walked along." Ellicott: "The words were on the point of rising to their lips as they looked up and saw the stone rolled away."

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson on their well-meant but unconsidered zeal: "as it is a holy zeal that blinds them, God does not charge them with this fault... let us learn from this, that in consequence of having been carried away by their zeal, they came there without due consideration. They had seen [the stone] placed before the sepulchre, to hinder any one from entering. Why did not this occur to them, when they were at home and at leisure, but because they were seized with such fear and astonishment, that thought and recollection failed them?"

Wesley notes their ignorance of the greater obstacle: "This seems to have been the only difficulty they apprehended. So they knew nothing of Pilate's having sealed the stone, and placed a guard of soldiers there." The women worried about the stone they knew of, unaware of the guard they did not — and both obstacles were already removed by the time they arrived. Henry (under v.1) drew it out: "see how by the time they came thither, both these difficulties were removed... They who are carried by a holy zeal, to seek Christ diligently, will find the difficulties that lie in their way strangely to vanish, and themselves helped over them beyond their expectation."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, peculiar to Mark. The witnesses agree: the women, anxious about who would roll away the great stone, came in holy but unconsidered zeal — a zeal God did not blame, though it betrayed their failure to expect the resurrection and their ignorance of the sealed stone and the guard. Their worry was needless, for the difficulty they feared (and the greater one they knew not of) was already removed. The verse teaches that those who seek Christ diligently in love will find their obstacles strangely vanish; the stone that troubled them was rolled away before they arrived (v.4). The women's question, recorded by Mark alone (perhaps from Peter's memory), both reveals their lack of resurrection-faith and sets off the more strikingly the discovery that met them — the open tomb, the obstacle gone, the way to the risen Lord prepared by an unseen hand.
4:
"And when they looked, they saw that the stone was rolled away: for it was very great." The witnesses note the surprising discovery and the textual placement of "for it was very great." Robertson paints the scene: "With downcast eyes and heavy hearts they had been walking up the hill. Mark has his frequent vivid dramatic present behold. Their problem is solved for the stone lies rolled back before their very eyes... Mark explains by the size of the stone this sudden and surprising sight right before their eyes." Barnes and Clarke note that the clause "for it was very great" belongs in sense with v.3: "Who shall roll us away the stone... for it was very great. And when they looked, they saw that the stone was rolled away." Ellicott, however, takes it as "St. Mark's explanation of his having used the word rolled away."

The discovery of the open tomb the witnesses read as the first sign of the resurrection. Matthew (28:2) tells that the stone was rolled away by an angel; Mark simply records the women's amazed finding. The very greatness of the stone — too great for the women to move (v.3) — magnifies the wonder of its removal, and points to the divine hand that had opened the way.

Bede (under v.1) read the rolling away mystically: "This rolling away of the stone means mystically the opening of the Christian Sacraments, which were held under the veil of the letter of the law; for the law was written on stone." The Peter Chrysologus material adds: "Great indeed by its office rather than its size, for it can shut in and throw open the body of the Lord."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the women, looking up, saw the great stone already rolled away — a sudden and surprising sight that solved the difficulty they had feared (v.3). The very greatness of the stone, beyond their strength to move, magnifies the wonder of its removal by the divine hand (the angel of Matthew 28:2). The open tomb was the first sign that met them, the threshold of the resurrection discovery. The fathers read the rolled-away stone as the opening of the mysteries once veiled under the law (written on stone). The verse marks the transition from the women's anxious approach to their entering the tomb (v.5) and hearing the angel's announcement. The obstacle gone, the way to the risen Lord lay open; what the women could not have done for themselves, God had already done — rolling away the stone not to let Christ out, but to let the witnesses in.
5:
"And entering into the sepulchre, they saw a young man sitting on the right side, clothed in a long white garment; and they were affrighted." The witnesses note the angelic messenger and the variations among the Gospels. Robertson: "A young man. An angel in Matthew 28:5, two men in Luke 24. These and like variations in details show the independence of the narrative and strengthen the evidence for the general fact of the resurrection." Ellicott: "So St. Mark describes the form which St. Matthew simply calls an angel of the Lord." Barnes notes the tomb "was large enough to admit persons to go into it; not unlike, in that respect, our vaults." Robertson explains "they were amazed... utterly amazed."

The Latin and Antiochene fathers read the young man richly. Gregory: "what is meant by the left hand, but this present life, and what by the right, but everlasting life? Because then our Redeemer had already gone through the decay of this present life, fitly did the Angel, who had come to announce His everlasting life, sit on the right hand." The Peter Chrysologus material: "they saw a young man sitting on the right, because the Resurrection has nothing sinister in it... dressed in a long white robe; that robe is not from mortal fleece, but of living virtue, blazing with heavenly light." The young man (not old, not infant) figures "the age of joy," for "the Resurrection has no old age."

The women's fright the witnesses read with tenderness. Henry (under v.1): "The sight of him might justly have encouraged them, but they were affrighted. Thus many times that which should be matter of comfort to us, through our own mistakes and misapprehensions proves a terror to us." The angelic messenger, meant to reassure, at first alarmed them.

The witnesses agree: the women, entering the tomb, saw an angel in the form of a young man, robed in white and seated on the right side, and were affrighted. The variation among the Gospels — a young man (Mark), an angel (Matthew), two men (Luke) — far from being a contradiction, shows the independence of the witnesses and strengthens the evidence for the resurrection itself. The fathers read the youth, the white robe, and the right-hand seat as figures of the resurrection's joy, glory, and everlasting life. The women's fright, at what should have comforted them, illustrates how the messages of grace may at first alarm a troubled heart — an alarm the angel at once moves to dispel (v.6). The empty tomb and the heavenly messenger together prepare the women for the announcement that Christ is risen.
6:
"And he saith unto them, Be not affrighted: Ye seek Jesus of Nazareth, which was crucified: he is risen; he is not here: behold the place where they laid him." The witnesses note the angel's reassurance and the great announcement. Robertson: "The angel noted their amazement and urges the cessation of it... The crucified one. This description of his shame has become his crown of glory, for Paul (Galatians 6:14), and for all who look to the Crucified and Risen Christ... He is risen — first aorist passive indicative, the simple fact." Clarke notes "Jesus of Nazareth" — the name of reproach become the name of honour: "Our Lord, by continuing to bear the name of the Nazarene, teaches us not to be too nice or scrupulous in fixing our own appellation."

The angel's words the puritan Henry (under v.1) unfolds: "Be not affrighted, for ye are faithful lovers of Jesus Christ... Ye seek Jesus of Nazareth, which was crucified." He notes "the enquiries of believing souls after Christ, have a particular regard to him as crucified" (1 Corinthians 2:2). And: "He was crucified in weakness, yet that doth not hinder but that he may be raised in power... He was crucified, but he is glorified; and the shame of his sufferings is so far from lessening the glory of his exaltation, that that glory perfectly wipes away all the reproach of his sufferings."

The proof offered — "behold the place where they laid him" — the witnesses note as the angel's appeal to the evidence of the empty tomb. The Peter Chrysologus material (under v.1): "This too was the reason why he had rolled away the stone, that he might shew them the place." The body was gone, "not stolen either by his enemies or by his friends, but risen" (Henry).

The witnesses agree: the angel stills the women's fear and proclaims the heart of the gospel — "He is risen; he is not here" — pointing to the empty place where the body had lain as the proof. The title "Jesus of Nazareth, which was crucified" joins the lowliness of his name and the shame of his cross to the glory of his rising: the Crucified is the Risen One, and his shame has become his crown. The angel's announcement, addressed to the faithful seekers of the crucified Jesus, turns their grief to the threshold of joy. The empty tomb, deliberately shown them, is the silent witness to the resurrection — the body neither stolen nor decayed, but raised in power. The verse is the centre of the chapter and of the gospel: He is risen.
7:
"But go your way, tell his disciples and Peter that he goeth before you into Galilee: there shall ye see him, as he said unto you." The witnesses dwell on the tender, special message to Peter. Gill notes Peter is named "not as distinct from the apostles... much less because he was the chief of them; but to comfort him in his great sorrow, on account of his fall," and observes that "this Gospel being published, as is thought by some, under the direction and examination of Peter himself, he was careful to relate every thing, that either aggravated his own crime, or illustrated the grace of God." Robertson: "Only in Mark, showing that Peter remembered gratefully this special message from the Risen Christ."

The naming of Peter the witnesses read as the height of Christ's mercy. Barnes: "It was proof of the kindness and mercy of the Lord Jesus. Peter, just before the death of Jesus, had denied him... It would have been right if the Lord Jesus had from that moment cast him off... But he loved him still." Wesley: "And Peter — though he so oft denied his Lord. What amazing goodness was this!" Clarke: Peter "had forfeited his discipleship... However, he is now a penitent: tell him that Jesus is risen... and is ready to heal his backsliding, and love him freely."

Henry (under v.1) drew out the comfort to the penitent: "Go to Peter by name, and tell him, he shall be as welcome as any of the rest... A sight of Christ will be very welcome to a true penitent, and a true penitent shall be very welcome to a sight of Christ, for there is joy in heaven concerning him." Pseudo-Jerome (under v.1): "He says specially unto Peter, because he had shewn himself unworthy of being a disciple... but past sins cease to hurt us when they cease to be pleasing to us."

The witnesses agree: the angel's message singles out Peter by name — a tender mercy to the penitent who had denied his Lord, assuring him that he is not cast off but still owned and loved by the risen Christ. The special naming, recorded by Mark alone (and gratefully remembered by Peter, whose preaching underlies this Gospel), displays the boundless grace of Christ toward the fallen who repent. To the disciples in their grief, and to Peter in his shame, comes the word that the risen Lord goes before them into Galilee, where they shall see him. The verse is the gospel's word to every true penitent: past sins, no longer loved, no longer condemn; the risen Christ welcomes the weeping disciple back. The message turns Peter's sorrow toward the joy of reunion with his Lord.
8:
"And they went out quickly, and fled from the sepulchre; for they trembled and were amazed: neither said they any thing to any man; for they were afraid." The witnesses note the women's trembling flight and silence, and (the grammatical voices) the great textual question that begins here. Robertson: "Trembling and astonishment (trembling and ecstasy), Mark has it, while Matthew 28:8 has with fear and great joy... Clearly and naturally their emotions were mixed... This excitement was too great for ordinary conversation... The continued fear explains their continued silence." Augustine (under v.1) had reconciled the apparent discrepancy with Matthew's "great joy": "both feelings were in their minds... that joy of which Matthew speaks is not inconsistent with the fear which Mark mentions."

The women's silence the witnesses read as the effect of their overwhelming fear. Henry (under v.1): "Our disquieting fears often hinder us from doing that service to Christ and to the souls of men, which if faith and the joy of faith were strong, we might do." Their failure to mix faith with Christ's predictions of rising left them terrified rather than triumphant.

The grammatical voices note that this verse is where the two oldest manuscripts end. Robertson lays out the evidence at length: "At this point Aleph and B, the two oldest and best Greek manuscripts of the New Testament, stop with this verse... The great mass of the documents have the long ending seen in the English versions... the facts are very complicated, but argue strongly against the genuineness of verses 9-20 of Mark 16... It is difficult to believe that Mark ended his Gospel with verse 8 unless he was interrupted. A leaf or column may have been torn off at the end of the papyrus roll."

The witnesses agree: the women fled the tomb trembling and amazed, their mixed fear and joy too great for speech, and at first said nothing for fear. Their silence, born of overwhelming emotion rather than disobedience, illustrates how disquieting fear can hinder the service of Christ. The verse is also the great textual crux of Mark: the two oldest manuscripts end here, and the genuineness of the longer ending (vv.9-20) is gravely doubted by many scholars, though the mass of manuscripts and the ancient fathers received it. Whether Mark ended at v.8 (interrupted, or the ending lost) or wrote on, the abrupt close on the women's awe leaves the reader at the threshold of the resurrection's full unfolding. The trembling silence of the first witnesses gives way, in the verses that follow, to the appearances of the risen Lord.
9:
"Now when Jesus was risen early the first day of the week, he appeared first to Mary Magdalene, out of whom he had cast seven devils." The witnesses note the first appearance and (the grammatical voices) the textual question of the longer ending. Robertson surveys the manuscript evidence and the unusual features: "Jesus is not mentioned by name here, though he is clearly the one meant... This description of Mary Magdalene is like that in Luke 8:2 and seems strange in Mark at this point, described as a new character here, though mentioned by Mark three times just before (15:40, 47; 16:1)." Clarke notes "this, to the conclusion of the Gospel, is wanting in the famous Codex Vaticanus, and has anciently been wanting in many others."

The Latin and Antiochene fathers, receiving the verses as Scripture, read them richly. Theophylact: "In the beginning also woman brought man into sin, now she, who first tasted death, first sees the Resurrection... she who had been the channel of guilt to man, now has become the first channel of grace." Bede: "Fitly too is this woman, who was the first to announce the joy of our Lord's Resurrection, said to have been cured of seven devils, lest any one worthily repenting of his sins should despair of pardon... where sin abounded, grace did much more abound" (Romans 5:20). Gregory: "by seven devils [is meant] all vices; for as by seven days is understood all time, so by the number seven a whole is fitly figured." He adds that the disciples' slowness to believe "was not so much a weakness of theirs as it is our strength," for the resurrection was thereby "manifested by many proofs."

The puritan Henry: "It was she out of whom he had cast seven devils; much was forgiven her, and much was given her... and she loved much; and this honour Christ did her, that she was the first that saw him after his resurrection. The closer we cleave to Christ, the sooner we may expect to see him."

The witnesses agree on the substance — that the risen Christ appeared first to Mary Magdalene, the forgiven woman of great love — while the grammatical voices register the grave doubt over the genuineness of the longer ending (vv.9-20), absent from the oldest manuscripts. The fathers, receiving the verses, read in Mary's priority a figure of grace abounding over sin: she who first tasted death's guilt becomes the first herald of life. The choice of one delivered from seven devils to be the first witness assures the repentant that no depth of past sin bars the way to pardon and to Christ. (On the textual question, see the note on the longer ending.) The chapter's close, whether by Mark's hand or an early addition, unfolds the appearances that confirm the resurrection announced at the empty tomb.
10:
"And she went and told them that had been with him, as they mourned and wept." The brief verse records Mary's report to the grieving disciples. Gill: "she went, as she was bid by Christ, and told his disciples, what she had heard and seen; even those who had been with him from the beginning... not only Peter, James, and John... but the rest of the eleven, and not only them, but others that were with them." Robertson notes the phrase "them that had been with him" is unusual: "This phrase for the disciples occurs here alone in Mark and the other Gospels... All these items suggest another hand than Mark for this closing portion."

The disciples' state — "as they mourned and wept" — the witnesses read with tenderness. Clarke: "Because they had lost their Lord and Master, and had basely abandoned him in his extremity." Robertson: "Present active participles... describing the pathos of the disciples in their utter bereavement and woe." Henry (under v.9): "Now was the time of which Christ had told them, that they should mourn and lament (John 16:20). And it was an evidence of their great love to Christ, and the deep sense they had of their loss of him."

Mary's errand the witnesses note as the fulfilment of her commission and a model of comfort. Henry: "Better news cannot be brought to disciples in tears, than to tell them of Christ's resurrection. And we should study to be comforters to disciples that are mourners, by communicating to them our experiences, and what we have seen of Christ." The forgiven woman, made the first herald, carries the good news to the weeping company.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Mary, obeying her commission, carried the news of the risen Christ to his mourning, weeping disciples — better news than which none could be brought to disciples in tears. Their grief, the fulfilment of Christ's prediction that they would mourn (John 16:20), was the evidence of their love and the sense of their loss. Mary's report models the duty of comforting mourners with the experience of Christ. The grammatical voices note the unusual phrasing ("them that had been with him") as one of the marks suggesting the longer ending may be by another hand (see the note on v.9). Yet the substance stands: the first herald of the resurrection brought its comfort to the grieving — though, as the next verse shows, they would not at first believe. The good news of the risen Lord is the true balm for disciples in tears.
11:
"And they, when they had heard that he was alive, and had been seen of her, believed not." The witnesses note the disciples' disbelief and its evidential value. Barnes: "This is proof that they did not expect his resurrection; proof that they were not easily deceived; and that nothing but the clearest evidence could undeceive them." Robertson notes the rare verb "disbelieved." Gill: "when they heard the report of Mary Magdalene, that Christ was raised from the dead, and was certainly alive."

The Reformed Calvin rebukes the disciples' culpable unbelief: "the disciples must have been held bound by shameful indifference, so that they did not recall to their recollection that what they had often heard from their Master was accomplished... they must have been uncommonly stupid in holding as a fable or a dream what had been so frequently promised and declared by the Son of God, when eye-witnesses assured them that it was accomplished... their unbelief having deprived them of sound understanding, they not only refuse the light of truth, but reject it." Their disbelief was not innocent caution but a failure to mix faith with Christ's plain predictions.

Yet the disbelief, the witnesses note, serves the evidence for the resurrection. Henry (under v.9): "There was a wise providence in it, the proofs of Christ's resurrection were given in thus gradually, and admitted thus cautiously, that so the assurance with which the apostles preached this doctrine afterward... might be the more satisfying. We have the more reason to believe those who did themselves believe so slowly: had they swallowed it presently, they might have been thought credulous." The very slowness of the disciples to believe strengthens the credibility of their later testimony.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the disciples, hearing Mary's report, did not believe — a disbelief that was, on one hand, culpable (Calvin: a shameful indifference to Christ's oft-repeated promise) and, on the other, providentially useful, since their slowness to be convinced strengthens the credibility of their later bold testimony. They were not credulous men easily deceived, but were undeceived only by the clearest evidence. The verse both rebukes the failure to mix faith with Christ's predictions and assures the reader that the resurrection rests on the testimony of men who themselves believed only upon full conviction. The disciples' initial unbelief, far from weakening the gospel, undergirds it: those who preached the risen Christ at the cost of their lives had first doubted, and were persuaded only by overwhelming proof.
12:
"After that he appeared in another form unto two of them, as they walked, and went into the country." The witnesses note the appearance to the two on the road to Emmaus. Robertson: "Luke tells us that it was on the same day. In another form. It was not a transfiguration like that described in 9:2. Luke explains that their eyes were holden so that they could not recognize Jesus (Luke 24:16). This matchless story appears in full in Luke 24:13-32." Barnes: "In a form unlike his ordinary appearance, so much so that they did not at first know him." Clarke identifies them: "These were the two who were going to Emmaus."

The phrase "in another form" the witnesses (with Augustine, under v.9) read carefully: "That which Mark expresses by the Lord's appearance in another form, is what Luke means by saying that their eyes were holden that they could not know Him." The Peter Chrysologus material guards against misreading: "let no one suppose that Christ changed the form of His face by His Resurrection, but the form is changed when of mortal it becomes immortal... He gained a glorious countenance, not that He lost the substance of His countenance." Henry (under v.9) agreed: "he had really his own countenance, appears by this, that their eyes were holden... and when that restraint on their eyes was taken off, immediately they knew him."

The fathers read the appearance "as they walked" mystically. Pseudo-Jerome (under v.9): "He was shewn to them as they were walking, that is, labouring, in another form... Here we see His face through a glass, there we shall see the truth face to face." The two on the road figure the believer's pilgrimage, seeing Christ now dimly, hereafter fully.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which summarizes Luke's Emmaus narrative. The witnesses agree: the risen Christ appeared to two disciples on the road to Emmaus "in another form" — not that his face was changed in substance, but that their eyes were held from recognizing him until he chose to reveal himself. The appearance, told in full by Luke (24:13-32), adds another proof of the resurrection. The fathers read the walking disciples as a figure of the believer's pilgrimage, who now sees Christ through a glass darkly and shall hereafter see him face to face. The verse continues the gradual unfolding of the resurrection appearances, by which the doubting disciples were brought to full conviction. The risen Lord, appearing now to one, now to two, now to the eleven, met his people in their grief and walked with them in their journey, opening the Scriptures and revealing himself in the breaking of bread.
13:
"And they went and told it unto the residue: neither believed they them." The witnesses note the renewed report and the renewed disbelief, and harmonize it with Luke. Gill: "Upon their return to Jerusalem... they went to the eleven apostles, and the other disciples that were with them, and related the whole affair." Robertson: "The men fared no better than the women. But Luke's report of the two on the way to Emmaus is to the effect that they met a hearty welcome by them in Jerusalem (Luke 24:33-35). This shows the independence of the two narratives... There was probably an element who still discredited all the resurrection stories."

The apparent discrepancy with Luke — who has the Eleven saying "The Lord is risen indeed, and hath appeared to Simon" (Luke 24:34) — Lightfoot resolves at length: "the reconciling of the evangelists is to be fetched thence, that those words... do not manifest their absolute confession of the resurrection of Christ, but a conjectural reasoning of the sudden and unexpected return of Peter." Wesley offers a simpler harmony: "They were moved a little by the testimony of these, added to that of St. Peter (Luke 24:34); but they did not yet fully believe it." The disbelief was not total but incomplete — a wavering between hope and doubt.

Henry (under v.9) drew out the providential value: "This did not gain credit with all... Now there was a wise providence in it, the proofs of Christ's resurrection were given in thus gradually, and admitted thus cautiously, that so the assurance with which the apostles preached this doctrine afterward... might be the more satisfying."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the two from Emmaus reported to the rest, and still they did not believe — or did not yet fully believe. The apparent tension with Luke's "The Lord is risen indeed" is resolved by taking the disciples' words there as a wavering, conjectural hope (kindled by Peter's report) rather than settled faith, which the two travellers' testimony did not yet fully establish. The repeated disbelief, far from weakening the gospel, displays the providential gradualness by which the resurrection was proved and cautiously admitted, so that the apostles' later confident preaching might rest on overwhelming conviction. The verse continues the pattern: report met with disbelief, until the risen Christ himself appeared to the eleven (v.14) and rebuked their unbelief. The slowness of the first believers undergirds the certainty of the faith they would afterward proclaim to the world.
14:
"Afterward he appeared unto the eleven as they sat at meat, and upbraided them with their unbelief and hardness of heart, because they believed not them which had seen him after he was risen." The witnesses note the appearance to the eleven and the rebuke of their unbelief. Robertson and Ellicott refer to the parallels (Luke 24:36-43; John 20:19-23). Augustine (under v.14) addresses the difficulty of "the last time" and the absence of Thomas, concluding that Mark "wished to say it in few words" and that the rebuke "must have been amongst those words which Luke and John say the Lord at that time spoke."

The Latin and Antiochene fathers read the rebuke and the commission. Pseudo-Jerome: "He rebukes their want of faith, that faith might take its place; He rebukes the hardness of their stony heart, that the fleshy heart, full of love, might take its place." Gregory: "Another reason also why our Lord rebuked His disciples... was, that the words which He spoke on leaving them might remain more deeply impressed upon the hearts of His hearers." Gregory notes the eating proved the reality of the flesh: "He ate, and then ascended, that by the act of eating, the truth of the flesh might be declared."

The puritan Henry (covering vv.14-18): Christ "upbraided them with their unbelief and hardness of heart." He draws the lesson: "while Christ comforts his people, he often sees it needful to rebuke and correct them for hardness of heart in distrusting his promise." The rebuke was an act of love, correcting the unbelief that had resisted the testimony of the witnesses.

The witnesses agree: the risen Christ appeared to the eleven at meat and rebuked their unbelief and hardness of heart — a rebuke of love, that faith might replace their unbelief and a heart of flesh their stony heart. The eating (Luke, Acts) proved the reality of his risen body; the rebuke pressed home the failure to believe the witnesses he had sent. Christ does not flatter his people but corrects them, that the lesson may be deeply impressed. The appearance to the eleven, the culminating resurrection appearance in Mark, both confirms the resurrection by his bodily presence and prepares for the Great Commission (v.15): the same disciples rebuked for unbelief are now sent to preach belief to all the world. The rebuke and the commission together show that Christ, having corrected his servants, entrusts them with his gospel — grace triumphing over their failure.
15:
"And he said unto them, Go ye into all the world, and preach the gospel to every creature." The witnesses note the Great Commission and its universal scope. Robertson: "This commission in Mark is probably another report of the missionary [charge] in Matthew 28:16-20 spoken on the mountain in Galilee." Ellicott: "Better, to the whole creation. The universality of the word is, of course, limited by the nature of the case." Barnes draws out the breadth: "To the Gentiles as well as the Jews. This was contrary to the opinions of the Jews... Jesus has graciously ordered that the preaching of the gospel shall be stopped by no barriers. Wherever there is man, there it is to be proclaimed. To every sinner he offers life; and all the world is included in the message of mercy."

The phrase "every creature" the witnesses interpret variously. Lightfoot, with rabbinic learning, notes the phrase commonly denoted "all men" and "especially the Gentiles." Gregory (under v.14) read it richly: "Every man must be understood by every creature; for man partakes something of every creature... The Gospel is preached to every creature, because He is taught by it, for whose sake all are created." Clarke takes it of "the Gentile world; for in this sense [the phrase] is often understood among the rabbins."

The universal commission the witnesses note as the great reversal of the earlier restriction. Gregory (under v.14): "it had been said before, Go not into the way of the Gentiles. But now it is said, Preach the Gospel to every creature." Wesley presses its breadth: "Our Lord speaks without any limitation or restriction. If therefore every creature in every age hath not heard it, either those who should have preached, or those who should have heard it, or both, made void the counsel of God."

The witnesses agree: the risen Christ commissions his disciples to preach the gospel to all the world, to every creature — a universal commission breaking down the partition between Jew and Gentile and extending the message of mercy to every human being. The earlier restriction ("Go not into the way of the Gentiles") is now reversed: wherever there is a sinner, the gospel is to be proclaimed and life offered. The Great Commission, the climax of the risen Lord's words in Mark, lays on the Church the perpetual duty of worldwide evangelism. The verse turns the disciples from doubters (v.14) into heralds, sending them to carry to all nations the good news of the crucified and risen Christ. The boundless scope of the commission matches the boundless mercy of the Saviour, who offers eternal life to all the world.
16:
"He that believeth and is baptized shall be saved; but he that believeth not shall be damned." The witnesses note the terms of salvation and debate the place of baptism. Robertson presses the key point: "The omission of baptized with disbelieveth would seem to show that Jesus does not make baptism essential to salvation. Condemnation rests on disbelief, not on baptism. So salvation rests on belief. Baptism is merely the picture of the new life not the means of securing it." Wesley agrees: "But he that believeth not — whether baptized or unbaptized, shall perish everlastingly." Ellicott softens the second clause: "Better, shall be condemned. The Greek word does not necessarily imply the idea of irreversible endless condemnation."

The Reformed Calvin gives the careful balance: baptism "is joined to the faith of the gospel, in order to inform us that the Mark of our salvation is engraved on it... Yet, at the same time, we must hold that it is not required as absolutely necessary to salvation, so that all who have not obtained it must perish; for it is not added to faith, as if it were the half of the cause of our salvation, but as a testimony." He concludes: "it is not necessary in itself, but only with respect to our obedience." Theophylact (under v.14) had noted: "it is not enough to believe, for he who believeth and is not baptized, but is a catechumen, has not yet attained to perfect salvation."

Barnes, by contrast, stresses baptism's importance: "Jesus has made baptism of so much importance, he did not say, indeed, that a man could not be saved without baptism, but he has strongly implied that where this is neglected, knowing it to be a command of the Saviour, it endangers the salvation of the soul." On the condemnation of unbelief, Barnes gives a full theological defence of its justice.

The witnesses agree on the terms — belief and baptism unto salvation, unbelief unto condemnation — while differing on baptism's precise place. Most (Robertson, Calvin, Wesley) note that the second clause omits baptism: condemnation rests on unbelief alone, not on want of baptism, so baptism is the sign and testimony of salvation, not its essential cause. Barnes stresses its importance though not its absolute necessity. All agree that saving faith is the decisive thing, and that unbelief — the deliberate rejection of the gospel — justly brings condemnation. The verse sets the solemn alternative before all who hear the Great Commission: to believe is to be saved, to refuse is to be condemned. Faith, attested by baptism, is the way of life; unbelief is the way of ruin.
17:
"And these signs shall follow them that believe; In my name shall they cast out devils; they shall speak with new tongues." The witnesses note the promised signs and debate whether they were temporary or perpetual. Barnes: "The promise is fulfilled if it can be shown that these signs followed in the case of any who believed, and it is not necessary to suppose that they would follow in the case of all... Miracles were necessary for the establishment of religion in the world; they are not necessary now." On "in my name," Barnes draws the contrast with Christ's own miracles: "He acted in his own name; they in the name of another... He was, therefore, God; they were men." The speaking with new tongues was "remarkably fulfilled on the day of Pentecost" (Acts 2:4-11).

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest treatment of the temporary view: "it is more probable that miracles were promised only for a time, in order to give luster to the gospel, while it was new and in a state of obscurity... we see that the use of them ceased not long afterwards." He warns that later forged miracles "opened the door for the impostures of Satan," and that Christ foretold the false signs of Antichrist (Matthew 24:24; 2 Thessalonians 2:9). Gill agrees the signs were "not always, only for a time; and which were necessary for the confirmation of the Gospel."

The Latin fathers read the signs spiritually for the ongoing Church. Gregory (under v.14): "Holy Church does every day in spirit what then the Apostles did in body... when her Priests by the grace of exorcism lay their hands on believers... what do they, but cast out devils? And the faithful who... sound forth the Holy Mysteries, speak a new language." Wesley holds a middle view: "in every believer faith has a latent miraculous power... Miracles, in the beginning, were helps to faith; now also they are the object of it."

The witnesses agree the signs accompanied the apostolic age while differing on their continuance. Most (Calvin, Gill, Barnes) hold they were temporary — given to confirm the gospel while new, and ceasing thereafter, lest forged miracles open the door to Satan's impostures. The fathers (Gregory) read an ongoing spiritual fulfilment in the Church's daily work. Wesley allows a latent miraculous power in faith still. All agree the signs were real, wrought "in Christ's name" (not the apostles' own), and served to authenticate the gospel — the casting out of demons and speaking with new tongues (Pentecost) being conspicuously fulfilled. The verse both confirms the apostolic preaching by attendant wonders and (in the prevailing reading) marks those wonders as the scaffolding of the infant Church, removed once the building stood.
18:
"They shall take up serpents; and if they drink any deadly thing, it shall not hurt them; they shall lay hands on the sick, and they shall recover." The witnesses note the signs and their New Testament fulfilments. Barnes: "They shall take up serpents. When it is necessary for the sake of establishing religion, they shall handle poisonous reptiles without injury... This was literally fulfilled when Paul shook the viper from his hand (Acts 28:5-6)." On the deadly drink and the sick: Barnes and Ellicott note the only recorded serpent instance is Paul at Melita, no New Testament instance of the deadly drink (though tradition tells of the poisoned cup offered to John), and the laying on of hands fulfilled in Acts (3:6-7; 5:15; 28:8; James 5:14-15). Clarke recounts the tradition that Mohammed, "who styled himself The Apostle of God, lost his life by poison," as a contrast.

The grammatical voices register the doubt over genuineness. Robertson: "Bruce considers these verses in Mark a great lapse from the high level of Matthew's version of the farewell words of Jesus... The great doubt concerning the genuineness of these verses (fairly conclusive proof against them in my opinion) renders it unwise to take these verses as the foundation for doctrine or practice unless supported by other and genuine portions of the New Testament." Wesley adds a wise caution against presumption: "If they drink any deadly thing — but not by their own choice. God never calls us to try any such experiments."

The signs the witnesses read as promises of divine protection and power in the establishing of the gospel, not warrants for presumptuous testing. The taking up of serpents (Paul at Malta) and the healing of the sick (the apostles in Acts) authenticated the apostolic mission; the promise was that God would guard his heralds and work through their hands while the gospel was being planted.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the promised signs — immunity to serpents and poison, healing of the sick — were fulfilled in the apostolic age (Paul's viper, Acts 28; the healings of Acts) as authentications of the gospel, displaying God's protection of his heralds and power through their hands. Wesley wisely cautions that the promise is no warrant for presumptuous experiment: "God never calls us to try any such experiments." The grammatical voices note the grave doubt over the genuineness of these verses, urging that they not be made a foundation for doctrine or practice apart from the clear teaching of the rest of the New Testament. The signs, whether read as apostolic credentials now ceased (the prevailing view) or otherwise, point to the divine power that accompanied the planting of the Church — God working with his servants (v.20) to confirm the word they preached.
19:
"So then after the Lord had spoken unto them, he was received up into heaven, and sat on the right hand of God." The witnesses note the ascension and the session at God's right hand. Robertson: "Luke gives the fact of the Ascension twice (Luke 24:50f.; Acts 1:9-11)... We should not know when it took place nor where but for Luke who locates it on Olivet at the close of the forty days." On the session: "Swete notes that the author passes beyond the field of history into that of theology," citing the New Testament's many references (Acts 7:55; Romans 8:34; Ephesians 1:20; Hebrews 1:3; 1 Peter 3:22; Revelation 3:21).

The Latin and Antiochene fathers read the ascension and session richly. Gregory contrasts Christ with Elijah: "Elias mounted up in a chariot, that by this they might understand that a mere man requires help from without. But our Redeemer... was borne over all by His own power." On the session: "sitting is the attitude of a judge, standing of one fighting or helping... Mark describes Him as sitting after His assumption into heaven, because after the glory of His assumption, He will in the end be seen as a judge." Augustine guards against crude literalism: "Let us not understand this sitting as though He were placed there in human limbs... by the right hand itself we understand the power which He as man received from God."

The puritan Henry: "After the Lord had spoken what he had to say to his disciples, he went up into heaven... he had not only an admission, but an abundant entrance, into his kingdom there; he was received in state... Sitting in a posture of rest, for now he had finished his work, and a posture of rule, for now he took possession of his kingdom... Now he is glorified with the glory he had before the world." Barnes: "to be seated at the right hand of God, means only that Jesus is exalted to the highest honor of the universe."

The witnesses agree: the risen Christ, having given his commission, ascended into heaven and sat at the right hand of God — the climax of his exaltation. He ascended by his own power (unlike Elijah's chariot), entering his kingdom in triumph; and the session at God's right hand signifies both rest (his work finished) and rule (his kingdom possessed), the highest honour and universal sovereignty. The "right hand" is not a place of bodily limbs but the power and majesty he received as man. Christ now reigns, glorified with the glory he had before the world, interceding for his people and awaiting the day he shall come as Judge. The ascension crowns the resurrection: the One raised from the tomb is exalted to the throne, that whatever God does for us, gives to us, or accepts from us, is by his Son.
20:
"And they went forth, and preached every where, the Lord working with them, and confirming the word with signs following. Amen." The witnesses note the apostles' obedient labour and Christ's effectual working with them. Barnes: "They went forth... In all parts of the world. The Lord working with them. By miracles; by removing obstacles; by supporting them, and by giving the gospel success... Confirming the word. Showing it to be the word of God." Robertson notes the genitive absolute "the Lord working with them" and that several words here "[are] not in Gospels elsewhere," consistent with the longer ending's distinct character.

The Reformed and grammatical voices stress the divine cooperation. Clarke: "This co-operation was twofold, internal and external. Internal, illuminating their minds... External, conveying their word to the souls that heard it... Though miraculous powers are not now requisite... yet this co-operation of God is indispensably necessary, without which no man can be a successful preacher." Henry (under v.19): "We have here the apostles working diligently for him; they went forth, and preached every where... and God working effectually with them, to make their labours successful, by confirming the word with signs following."

The signs that "followed" the witnesses note as the seals of the gospel. Henry: "the reformation of the world, the destruction of idolatry, the conversion of sinners, the comfort of saints; and these signs still follow it." Barnes draws the abiding lesson: "The effects of the gospel, when faithfully preached, and truly believed, in changing the tempers and characters of mankind, form a constant proof, a miraculous proof, that the gospel is the power of God unto salvation." On "Amen," Barnes and Clarke note it was likely a later addition, not part of Mark's original.

The witnesses agree: the apostles, obeying the commission, went forth and preached everywhere, and the Lord worked with them — confirming the word by attendant signs and giving the gospel success. The verse displays the divine-human cooperation in the spread of the gospel: the apostles labouring, Christ from his throne working effectually, both by outward miracles (the seals of the apostolic word) and by the inward power of the Spirit converting souls. Though the miraculous signs of the apostolic age have ceased, the cooperation of the exalted Christ with faithful preaching continues, and the transforming effects of the gospel remain its standing proof. The chapter, and the Gospel, close with the risen and ascended Lord no longer bodily present but mightily present from heaven, working with his Church as she carries his word to the ends of the earth. The "Amen" seals the whole: so be it, and so it is.
